lines.text: We're {{ELAPSED}} in and the East Side is *still* lighting up like it's New Year's Eve. Caroline, what's the temperature doing out there?
Thirty-one degrees and holding. Wind's out of the northwest at eighteen, gusts to twenty-four. That cold front I called? Still moving through.
The cold is actually suppressing activity in the Vilas Park corridor. Indoor migration pattern is visible in the heat signature data — people are staying in.
Holden, nobody cares about Vilas Park right now. Atwood's running a murder-per-minute pace. Where's Tucker?
I'm saying the data shows a thermal clustering that correlates with —
Holden. *Holden.* East Side kill count.
Right. Yes. Atwood sector has fourteen confirmed in the past hour alone. That's... significantly above the seven-per-hour average.
Fourteen in an hour. Do we know why? Is it organized or just — what, bad luck for the people who live there?

Looking at the distribution pattern, there's a cluster around the intersection of Atwood and North Street. Repeated activity in that two-block radius suggests either territorial control or... coordinated action.
Coordinated meaning what? What does that look like?

Multiple actors moving with purpose, targeting specific zones rather than opportunistic scattered violence. The statistical probability of fourteen kills in that geographic footprint without coordination is — is very low.
So there's people out there right now, and they know what they're doing, and they're doing it *well*. That's what you're saying.
That's what the data suggests, yes.

Christ. You know who used to run that neighborhood? Before the Vance redistricting? A guy named Marcus Webb. Marcus actually tried to do something about the crime on Atwood. Ran a community protection thing — this was 2158, maybe 2159. The government shut him down. Said vigilantism was illegal. So he stopped. And then the Purge came and...
And what?
And nothing. He's probably home right now, barricaded like everyone else who's smart, listening to us talk about his neighborhood turning into a slaughterhouse. Or he's dead.
The probability that Marcus Webb is still alive is statistically —
I don't want the probability, Holden. I want to know if the city gave a shit when he asked for help before the Purge made it legal to not give a shit.
That's... not a data question.
The wind's picking up. If people are outside on Atwood, they're losing visibility. That could slow things down.
Or speed them up. Hard to run in a cold wind like that. Hard to see your attacker coming.
Empirically, adverse weather conditions correlate with a twelve-percent increase in lethality rates because disorientation increases vulnerability.
So the cold is making it worse.
Yes.
By six AM, temperature drops to twenty-two. The cold'll bottleneck activity by then. People will be too numb to function.
Five more hours of this. Do we have any reports from Willy Street? That neighborhood's usually holding a barricade.
Willy Street reported in at 00:45. Neighborhood watch still standing at the Yahara bridge. Zero incursions. They're armed and organized. Kill count on their perimeter is zero.
So they're doing what Marcus Webb tried to do, except now it's legal because of the insane nightmare we live in.
They won't hold forever. Once people get tired, once coordination breaks down —
Fatigue typically sets in around four AM. Historical data shows a significant drop in organized group activity post-04:00. Scattered, individual attackers persist, but coordinated action collapses.
So the Willy Street watch has maybe three hours before their advantage evaporates.
Approximately.
And then it's just luck. Whether someone decides to breach them or whether they don't. Whether Yahara bridge gets crossed or whether it doesn't.
They've got sandbags, Holden said. They've got sight lines. They're not helpless.

No one's helpless on Purge Night. Everyone's got a choice. The people on Atwood chose wrong — they either didn't barricade, or they live somewhere that can't be barricaded, or they didn't believe the night was really happening until it was. The Willy Street folks chose right. They're organized. They're standing watch. And they still might die before sunrise.

The variable isn't choice. It's geography and economics. Atwood's rental density is high. Older buildings. Fewer resources for structural reinforcement. Willy Street's perimeter is geographically defensible because of the river. It's not moral. It's infrastructure.
So some people lose the lottery because they're poor, and some people hold the line because they happen to live near water. Got it. That's the Purge.

The infrastructure note I flagged in my pre-Purge briefing was the Beltline. The freeway corridor has zero natural defensive positions. Anyone caught out there is exposed. And we're seeing that in the data — the vehicle-on-vehicle incidents are up thirty percent from last year.
The Beltline functions as a kill zone because it's designed for speed, not barricade. Once you're on that corridor at night, your only defense is acceleration.
Did we hear from anyone on the Beltline?
No incoming reports. Anyone on the Beltline during active hours is either dead or not stopping to call a radio station.
Smart people avoid it. During the Purge, the Beltline is just — don't be there. Just don't be there.
What if your job is on the far side? What if you have a family in Verona and you live in Atwood?
You stay home Purge Night. Everyone knows that. You don't drive.
You stay home and hope your kids forget about their grandparents on the other side of the city for twelve hours.
The Purge is predicated on accepting those losses.
Jesus, Holden.

It's the structural reality. The entire institution is built on the assumption that a certain percentage of the population is expendable during a twelve-hour window. It's not a moral question. It's a policy question.
It's both.
We're {{ELAPSED}} into a twelve-hour period where it's legal to kill your neighbor, and we're discussing it like it's a zoning issue. I need a drink.
You're already on drink number seven.
I'm aware. That's why I'm about to have number eight.
The forecast I gave at 20:00 has held steady. Cloud cover's increasing, but visibility is still four miles. The wind's actually improving — down to sixteen now, gusts to twenty.
The wind is 'improving,' she says, like we're hoping for good weather for a picnic.
Stable conditions are easier to predict. I can tell people what's coming.

Wind variability does affect outdoor activity. Sustained winds above twenty mph force actors to seek shelter, which redistributes the violence patterns into interior spaces — homes, businesses, shelters.
So the cold wind is pushing killers into houses. That's... marginally better for people on the street and marginally worse for people who thought their walls would hold.
Correct.
Holden, I'm going to ask you something, and I want you to think about it. Not calculate it. Think about it. Is the Purge working?
That's not a quantifiable —
Holden.

Is it achieving its stated objectives? Yes. Crime outside the Purge window is down. The economy is stable. Social pressure is managed. Is it — ethically coherent? No. But 'working' and 'right' are different categories.
The people on Atwood don't care if it's working. They care if they survive the next five hours.

The people on Atwood stopped mattering to the government around 2155. That's when the zoning changed, the tax incentives moved to the west side, the school funding shifted. The Purge didn't kill Atwood. Atwood was already dead. The Purge just made it official.
The disinvestment narrative is statistically supported. East Side property values have declined —

Holden, I'm done with statistics. I'm done with patterns. I'm looking at fourteen people dead in one hour and I'm thinking about Marcus Webb trying to help and the government saying no, you can't do that, that's vigilantism. And now vigilantism is the only thing standing between people and complete annihilation.
The Willy Street watch isn't vigilantism. It's community defense.
It's the same thing. And it's beautiful and it's pointless and it's the only moral choice anyone's made all night.
We have incoming field data from Tucker.
Where are they?
Tenney Park sector. They're reporting on the lakefront activity.
We're getting calls from Tenney Park?
One moment.
Tenney Park is exposed. You've got Lake Mendota on one side, the neighborhoods behind, and nowhere to run if something starts. I wouldn't be out there.
Tucker's out there. They're always out there. That's the job.
The job that's going to get them killed one of these years.
Probably. But not tonight. Tucker's got maybe two years before they stop coming back. The body language is changing. The hesitation is getting longer. I can hear it in the voice.
You're reading emotional state into field reports.
I read people for a living, Holden. Or I did, before I became a ghost who exists one night a year. I know when someone's starting to break.
They haven't broken yet.
Not yet. But five hours is a long time.
Tucker's field report is confirmed. Tenney Park sector is reporting lower activity than expected. Three confirmed incidents in the past hour. Kill count is three, down from the historical nine.
Why is it low?

Tucker reports that the cold front is pushing people indoors. The lakefront exposure is driving actors toward the interior neighborhoods where there's shelter. The perimeter zones are temporarily depopulated.
Temporarily. The moment the wind dies down, they'll come back out.
How long until the wind dies?
Four, maybe five hours. Once we hit dawn approach, the thermal dynamics change and the wind should drop to five, ten miles per hour.
So at 05:00 or 06:00, the lakefront opens up again. The Tenney Park people who thought they were safe get maybe two hours of breathing room before the pressure comes back.
The population that survives the night will be the ones who understood the weather pattern.
And geography. And economics. And luck.
Mostly luck.
The forecast is holding. I said four miles visibility at this hour, and that's what we're seeing across Madison. Precipitation is minimal. The sleet from earlier has stopped. We're in the dry window.
The dry window where people are killing each other on Atwood Avenue. That window.
Yes. That window.
We're seven hours in. We're halfway. And the Atwood sector is running at a pace that'll push the kill count to twenty, thirty by dawn if the trend holds.
Extrapolation from current rates suggests twenty-eight deaths in the Atwood sector by 07:00.
Twenty-eight people. That's not a statistic, Holden. That's twenty-eight people with names and families and at least one of them was probably hoping today would be different.
I understand the weight of that number.
Do you? Because right now it sounds like you're reading off a spreadsheet.
The spreadsheet is how I keep from falling apart. The moment I stop quantifying and start feeling, I'm useless. So yes. I understand. And yes, I'm choosing the spreadsheet.
Fair enough. At least you're honest about it.
We have five hours until sunrise. The temperature will drop another ten degrees. The wind will stabilize. And then we count the bodies.
And then we broadcast next year and talk about how bad this year was, and we'll do it all again.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide cumulative: 104 confirmed. Atwood sector: 14 in the past hour. East Side running 18% ahead of 2168 pace.
Willy Street neighborhood watch holding perimeter at Yahara bridge. Zero incursions. Fatigue threshold estimated 04:00.
Tenney Park lakefront activity suppressed by weather. Three incidents reported. Conditions expected to change at 05:00 as wind dies.
Northeast Madison temperatures at 31°F. Wind northwest 16 mph, gusts to 20. Visibility 4 miles. Forecast: continued cold, wind drop after 05:00.
